title: Benin Bronzes
summary: Thousands of brass and bronze plaques, heads, and figures cast by the Edo people for the royal court of the Kingdom of Benin, in present-day Edo State, Nigeria, from at least the sixteenth century, within a court casting tradition whose roots may reach earlier. Made largely by lost-wax casting in brass for the palace of the Oba, they recorded court history and ritual in metal. In 1897 a British military expedition sacked Benin City and looted the works, which were sold across Europe and America. They are now the central case in the global debate over returning objects taken in the colonial era.
hook: A reader walking through a great European museum in 1900 would have met a quiet contradiction. On the wall hung brass plaques of astonishing control: figures in deep relief, drapery and coral regalia rendered with a jeweller's precision. The labels often called the people who made them primitive. The objects refused the label. They had been cast by a method as exacting as anything in Renaissance Florence, in a West African city most European visitors had never seen, by a kingdom Britain had burned three years earlier to take exactly these things.

beginner.theWork:
Picture a polished brass plaque about the size of a large book. On it stands a king in deep relief. He wears a high collar of coral beads and holds the symbols of his rule. Smaller figures attend him on either side. The background is filled with tiny patterns, like a woven cloth turned to metal. The casting is clean and confident. Nothing about it looks rushed or rough.

This is one of the Benin Bronzes. The name covers thousands of objects: flat plaques, human heads, animals, bells, and carved ivory tusks. Most are not pure bronze at all. They are brass. The Edo people made them for the royal court of the Kingdom of Benin. That kingdom sat in what is now southern Nigeria, in Edo State. It is not the same place as the modern country called Benin next door.

The oldest pieces date back roughly to the 13th century. They were made for the palace of the king, called the Oba. They were not decoration alone. They were records. The plaques showed past kings, great battles, court rituals, and the order of who stood where. A kingdom without much writing kept its history in metal instead.
beginner.momentOfMaking:
The metalworkers of Benin used a method called lost-wax casting. First an artist shaped the figure in beeswax. Then they covered the wax in clay and baked it hard. The wax melted and ran out, leaving a hollow mold. Hot liquid brass was poured into that space. When the metal cooled, the clay was broken away. What remained was a single solid casting, full of fine detail.

This was difficult, skilled work. The casters belonged to a special guild that served the palace directly. The skill passed from parent to child across many generations. The brass itself came partly through trade. European ships brought brass to the coast in the form of bracelets called manillas, often exchanged for goods, including enslaved people. Benin melted that metal down and made it into art for its kings.

The objects covered the palace. Plaques were once nailed to wooden pillars in the royal courtyards. Heads of past Obas sat on altars. Together they formed a kind of living archive that the court could read and honor.
beginner.whatItDoes:
The Bronzes do two jobs at once, and that is the heart of why they matter. They are works of art. They are also stolen property. Both things are true together.

As art, they shocked the people who first saw them in Europe. Many Europeans in 1900 believed that Africans had made nothing fine or complex. The Bronzes proved that belief false on sight. Here was court art as careful as anything from Europe, made centuries earlier in a city those visitors had dismissed.

As property, they carry a darker history. In 1897 a British armed force attacked Benin City. The soldiers burned the palace and forced the Oba into exile. They carried off the brasses and ivories by the thousand. The British government then sold many of the objects to pay for the cost of the attack. That is how the Bronzes ended up scattered through museums in London, Berlin, New York, and far beyond. A reader can admire the skill and still ask a plain question. Who should hold these things now?
beginner.whatItChanged:
For a long time the Bronzes sat in glass cases far from home. They taught European visitors about African skill. They also stood as quiet trophies of conquest. Most labels said little about how they had been taken.

That silence has broken. Today the Benin Bronzes are the most famous example in a worldwide argument about returning looted objects. Nigeria has asked for them back for decades. The mood has slowly shifted in its favor.

In 2022 Germany agreed to return ownership of many Bronzes held in its museums. Some pieces have since traveled back to Nigeria. Plans were announced for a new museum in Benin City to house returning works. Other museums, including the British Museum in London, have moved more slowly or refused.

The story is not simple even inside Nigeria. People there have argued over who should receive the returned objects. Should they go to the national government, or to the Oba of Benin, whose ancestors owned them? The Bronzes left as loot. Their return has become a question about history, ownership, and who speaks for the past.

general.theWork:
A brass plaque from Benin rewards slow looking. A king stands at the center in heavy coral regalia, flanked by attendants, the whole surface crowded with fine pattern and confident relief. The Edo casters who made such objects worked for the royal court of the Kingdom of Benin, in what is now Edo State in southern Nigeria, from at least the sixteenth century onward, in a casting tradition whose roots reach earlier. The pieces we call the Benin Bronzes are mostly brass rather than true bronze, and they include heads, figures, bells, and ivory alongside the famous plaques. They were not loose ornaments. They lined the pillars and altars of the palace and functioned as a court record, holding the memory of past kings, wars, and rituals in a court that preserved its record through image and ritual as much as through writing.

The casting method was lost-wax, the same technique used in Renaissance Italy. An artist modeled a figure in beeswax, encased it in clay, melted out the wax, and poured molten brass into the void. The brass came in part from Europe, traded to the coast as bracelet-shaped ingots known as manillas, some of them exchanged in the Atlantic slave trade. Benin absorbed that imported metal and turned it into images of its own power.
general.momentOfMaking:
The casters of Benin belonged to a guild that served the Oba directly, and their craft passed down through families across generations. The quality of the work was not accidental. It reflected centuries of accumulated skill, a steady supply of metal through long-distance trade, and a court wealthy enough to commission art on a large scale. To a European eye in 1900, none of this was supposed to exist. The dominant view held that sub-Saharan Africa had produced no sophisticated court tradition. The Bronzes were physical proof to the contrary, and that proof was unsettling rather than welcome.

The way the objects reached Europe was violent. In 1897 a British force mounted what it called a punitive expedition against Benin, following the killing of an earlier British party that had ignored a warning to stay away during a religious period. The expedition sacked Benin City, burned the royal palace, and sent the Oba into exile. Soldiers and officials removed thousands of brasses and ivories. The British government auctioned many of them in London, partly to cover the expedition's costs. Through that sale and later dealers, the objects spread to museums across Europe and North America, where they would sit, admired and rarely explained, for most of the next century.
general.whatItDoes:
The Benin Bronzes have always carried two arguments at once, and the tension between them is the point. They are extraordinary art, and they are evidence of a crime. Each reading tends to crowd out the other, which is why the objects remain so charged.

As art, they forced a reckoning with European assumptions. The refinement of the casting was difficult to square with the racial theories common around 1900, which placed African societies low on an imagined ladder of civilization. Some early European writers tried to explain the skill away, suggesting Portuguese or even ancient Egyptian influence rather than crediting Edo artists. That instinct to look elsewhere for the source is itself a piece of the historical record, and later scholarship has firmly rejected it.

As property, the Bronzes are the clearest case of cultural loot in the world. Because the taking is so well documented, and because the objects are so obviously valuable, they have become the test case for a much larger question. What does a museum owe when its prized holdings arrived through conquest? The Bronzes make that question impossible to wave away, because no one can pretend they were bought fairly or dug from neutral ground.
general.whatItChanged:
For most of the 20th century the Bronzes were displayed as masterpieces with their origins muted. Wall labels praised the craft and said little about the burning of Benin City. That arrangement has come apart. Nigeria has formally requested the objects' return since at least the 1930s, and the pressure has grown sharper in recent decades as former colonies pressed broader claims for restitution.

The pace changed in the 2010s and 2020s. A 2018 report commissioned by the French president argued that African heritage taken without consent should be returned, and it named Benin material specifically. In 2022 Germany transferred ownership of more than a thousand Bronzes in its public collections to Nigeria, with some physically returning to Lagos and Benin City. Other returns followed, including 119 objects from the Netherlands in 2025. A new museum was planned in Benin City to house them. The British Museum, bound by law that restricts what it can give away, has so far declined to transfer ownership, offering loans instead.

The debate inside Nigeria has its own sharp edges. After Germany's transfer, a 2023 declaration recognized the Oba of Benin as the rightful custodian of the returning works, which complicated earlier assumptions that they would enter federal or state museums. Who is the proper heir to looted royal art, the modern nation-state or the restored royal house, is now part of the argument. The objects that left as plunder have come home into a live dispute about ownership and memory.

advanced.theWork: The scholarly value of the Benin corpus rests on a feature that the 1897 looting both nearly destroyed and partly preserved. The plaques constitute a dense visual record of court hierarchy, dress, and ceremony. No written equivalent survives from within the kingdom. The British removed the objects from their architectural setting and dispersed them globally. As a result, the link between individual plaques and their original placement on the palace pillars is largely irrecoverable. Reconstructing the iconographic program now depends on fragmentary photographs, the testimony of Edo court historians, and comparison across collections that the dispersal itself fragmented. The interpretive problem is acute. The objects are at once the best evidence for Benin court culture and evidence whose context was shattered by the act that made them famous in Europe. Any reading of their meaning must therefore work against the grain of how they were taken.
advanced.momentOfMaking: The chronology and metallurgy of the Bronzes remain genuinely contested at the edges. Earlier scholars proposed stylistic sequences that sorted the heads and plaques into early, middle, and late phases. Chemical analysis of the brass has complicated those sequences. The metal supply, it suggests, shifted as Atlantic trade intensified after the late 15th century. The plaques themselves are now commonly dated to a concentrated period between roughly the 16th and 17th centuries, later than once assumed, while the casting tradition reaches back further. The implication is uncomfortable. The most celebrated portion of the corpus is bound up with the very Atlantic commercial system that European narratives long treated as separate from African artistic achievement. That system included the trade in enslaved people through which the manilla brass arrived. The art and the trade that supplied its material cannot be cleanly disentangled, and recent work treats that entanglement as central rather than embarrassing.
advanced.whatItDoes: The Bronzes occupy a peculiar position in the intellectual history of how Europe understood Africa. Their discovery by the wider European public in 1897 coincided with the high tide of scientific racism, and the immediate response was a search for non-African origins. The proposed explanations, Portuguese tuition or diffusion from ancient Egypt, were not idle speculation but a defensive maneuver to protect a racial hierarchy that the objects threatened. The eventual scholarly consensus that the Bronzes are entirely an Edo achievement was therefore not merely an art-historical correction but a dismantling of a broader epistemic structure. The deeper analytical point is that the objects did double work: they entered European collections as trophies confirming conquest, and they simultaneously functioned as counter-evidence to the ideology that justified conquest. That contradiction was never resolved within the museums; it was managed by display conventions that foregrounded craft and suppressed provenance.
advanced.whatItChanged: The restitution debate the Bronzes now anchor has exposed a fault line that the language of return tends to paper over. The dominant framing pits European museums against an African nation. The harder question concerns who, within Nigeria, holds rightful claim. The 2023 recognition of the Oba of Benin as custodian disrupted the assumption that restitution flows naturally to the nation-state. This is not a marginal complication. It raises a foundational problem about the appropriate unit of cultural ownership. Is it the modern state, the dynastic institution whose ancestors commissioned and owned the works, or a community defined some other way? The three answers point to materially different futures for the objects. Dan Hicks has argued that the focus on individual returns risks obscuring the organized character of the original violence. Others contend that custodial disputes are precisely the sovereign questions that restitution should hand back to Nigerians to resolve. The unresolved tension is whether restitution repairs a historical wrong or merely relocates an older set of contests over who may speak for Benin.

foundation.theStory: The Benin Bronzes pose a historical problem with two faces. On one side is a question of art and knowledge: how a West African kingdom developed a casting tradition of remarkable sophistication, and why Europeans found that fact so difficult to accept. On the other is a question of justice: how those objects came to sit in European and American museums, and what is owed now that the taking is no longer concealed. Understanding the Bronzes means holding both faces at once. They are among the finest works of court art produced anywhere in the medieval and early modern world, and they are among the most thoroughly documented cases of colonial plunder. Neither description cancels the other.
foundation.theDebate: Two debates organize work on the Bronzes. The first is historical and largely settled: the objects are entirely an Edo achievement, and the older attempts to credit Portuguese or Egyptian influence have been rejected as expressions of racial assumption rather than evidence. The second is live and unresolved: the question of restitution, where museums, the Nigerian state, and the royal court of Benin hold different positions. A newer thread asks not whether the objects should return but to whom within Nigeria they should return, and that question has sharpened since 2023.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Bronzes unlock the history of the Kingdom of Benin and the wider story of West African states before colonization. They open onto the British Empire's late expansion in Africa, the workings of the museum as an institution shaped by conquest, and the global restitution movement now reshaping major collections. They also illuminate how racial ideas distorted European understanding of Africa, because the objects were both proof against those ideas and trophies of the violence the ideas excused.
questions: Europeans around 1900 often refused to believe that Edo artists had made the Bronzes, proposing Portuguese or Egyptian sources instead. What does that refusal reveal about how knowledge and prejudice interacted, and why did the truth take so long to be accepted?

The Bronzes are both great art and stolen property. When a museum displays them, does emphasizing their beauty help honor their makers, or does it distract from how they were taken? Can a display do both honestly?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The recent conversation is dominated by the restitution turn. Dan Hicks's account frames the 1897 expedition as systematic colonial violence and challenges museums to confront it rather than manage it through display. Felwine Sarr and Bénédicte Savoy's report for the French government argued for returning African heritage taken without consent and reset the terms of the European debate. Barbara Plankensteiner's exhibition scholarship reconstructed the corpus across scattered collections and helped move the field toward collaborative work with Nigerian institutions. Against these, more cautious curators stress the legal and practical constraints on national museums and the value of objects remaining accessible to global audiences.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for the 1897 looting and the subsequent sale is strong: military records, auction catalogues, museum acquisition files, and photographs document the dispersal in unusual detail. The art-historical evidence for Edo authorship is conclusive. What the evidence cannot fully recover is the original placement and intended reading of individual plaques, because the British removed them from the palace architecture and scattered them before any systematic record of their arrangement was made. The internal Nigerian question of rightful custody is not an evidentiary problem at all but a political and legal one, which scholarship can clarify but not settle.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong position on the Bronzes must do more than condemn the looting, which is no longer seriously defended. It must engage the harder question that restitution has opened: to whom the objects properly return. One defensible argument runs that because the Bronzes were commissioned and owned by the Obas of Benin, the restored royal house has the strongest claim, and the 2023 recognition of the Oba as custodian follows from that history. A competing argument holds that the modern Nigerian state, as the sovereign successor, is the proper recipient and steward for the public. Choosing between them requires deciding whether cultural ownership tracks dynastic continuity or national sovereignty, and a serious argument must defend that choice rather than assume it.
questions: Dan Hicks argues that focusing on individual object returns can obscure the structural violence of the 1897 expedition. Does breaking restitution into case-by-case returns risk losing sight of the larger wrong, or is incremental return the only practical path?
If the Oba of Benin and the Nigerian federal government both claim the returning Bronzes, which principle should govern, dynastic ownership or national sovereignty, and what does each choice cost?

research.topic: The 1897 expedition and the dispersal of the objects
research.content: The British punitive expedition of February 1897, the sack of Benin City, the exile of Oba Ovonramwen, and the removal of thousands of brasses and ivories are documented through military records, participants' accounts, auction catalogues, and museum acquisition files. That the British government sold a large portion of the objects, partly to defray the expedition's costs, is established. The objects' subsequent distribution across European and North American museums is well traced, and the Edo authorship of the works is no longer in serious scholarly doubt.

research.topic: Chronology, metallurgy, and the question of custody
research.content: Chemical analysis of the brass has revised earlier stylistic datings, with the bulk of the plaques now commonly assigned to roughly the 16th and 17th centuries and the metal supply linked to intensifying Atlantic trade, including manilla brass tied to the slave trade. Separately, the restitution process has surfaced an unresolved question about rightful custody within Nigeria, sharpened by the 2023 recognition of the Oba of Benin as custodian of returning works. Both threads are active and the subject of ongoing research and negotiation.

research.topic: The original arrangement and reading of the plaques
research.content: What evidence would resolve it: a systematic record of how the plaques were mounted on the palace pillars and how the court read them as a sequence. Why it is unavailable: the British removed the objects from their architectural setting and dispersed them in 1897 before any such record was made, and the palace itself was burned. What can be responsibly inferred: the plaques formed a court archive of kings, wars, and rituals, and comparison across collections plus the testimony of Edo court historians supports partial reconstruction of their iconography. What must remain unknown: the precise original placement and intended reading order of most individual plaques.

comparativeNarrative.name: Looted Royal Archive
comparativeNarrative.content: For the people of Benin and for many Nigerians, the Bronzes are not first of all museum treasures. They are the looted archive of a kingdom, taken in a single act of violence in 1897. The plaques and heads recorded the line of Obas and the order of the court, so their absence is a hole in a living history rather than a gap in a foreign collection. This is why the return matters so deeply, and why the question of who receives them inside Nigeria is so charged. The dispute between the federal government and the royal court is not a technicality. It reflects a real disagreement about whether the rightful heir to royal art is the modern nation or the restored royal house whose ancestors owned it.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Universal Collection
comparativeNarrative.content: Large European and American museums have long defended keeping the Bronzes. They appeal to the idea of a collection for all of humanity. In such a place, objects from many cultures sit together and reach a wide public. The Bronzes, they say, teach millions about Benin who will never travel to Nigeria. Breaking up these collections would shrink that shared access to world art. Critics give a blunt reply. The argument was built to justify holding objects that arrived through conquest. Access is no excuse for keeping stolen property. The legal limits on national museums are real. So is the suspicion that they offer a handy reason to delay. The case for keeping the works has weakened as the record of the looting has grown harder to ignore.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Shape of the Wrong
comparativeNarrative.content: A newer body of work treats the Bronzes as a way to see the museum clearly. Scholars such as Dan Hicks argue that these objects are not neutral art with a troubled past. They are evidence of organized colonial violence. To display them without that history, Hicks says, is itself a choice. Returns that happen one object at a time can soften the larger truth. A single great wrong becomes a series of polite handovers. Other scholars in the same broad camp push back. Slow, piece-by-piece return, they argue, is the only path that actually works, and custody disputes are for Nigerians to settle. What unites this view is a single insistence. The real question is what the museum is, not only where each object ends up.

comparativeSummary: The same objects look different depending on where one stands. To Edo and Nigerian voices, the Bronzes are a stolen royal archive. Their return reopens an old question about who now speaks for Benin. To the great museums, they were pieces of a collection for all humanity, serving a wide public. That argument has lost force as the looting grew harder to ignore. To restitution scholars, the Bronzes show how conquest built the museum itself, and quiet, piece-by-piece returns risk blurring that truth. Held together, the three views reveal that restitution is not one question but several. It is about ownership, about access, and about whether moving an object repairs a wrong or just carries an old argument to a new address.

rabbitHole.label: The Kingdom of Benin
rabbitHole.reason: The Bronzes were made by and for the Kingdom of Benin's royal court. Understanding the Edo kingdom, its Obas, and its place in West African history is necessary to grasp what the objects recorded and why their loss in 1897 cut so deep.

rabbitHole.label: Ife
rabbitHole.reason: The older Yoruba city of Ife developed the sophisticated lost-wax casting tradition from which Benin's metalwork descends. Ife's naturalistic brass and terracotta heads are the artistic ancestor of the Benin court style.

rabbitHole.label: The Aksum Stelae
rabbitHole.reason: The Aksum obelisk looted by Italy and returned to Ethiopia in 2008 is a parallel African restitution case. It shows the same arc of colonial-era seizure and long, contested return that the Bronzes now embody on a larger scale.

rabbitHole.label: The British Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The 1897 expedition that looted the Bronzes was an act of late British imperial expansion in West Africa. The objects' presence in London museums is a direct product of empire and a continuing reminder of how it operated.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: The Bronzes are the central case in the wider reckoning with colonial plunder of cultural property. They make concrete the broader pattern by which conquest transferred not only land and labor but heritage and memory.

rabbitHole.label: The Scramble for Africa
rabbitHole.reason: The late-19th-century partition of Africa created the imperial competition and the pretexts under which Britain moved against Benin in 1897. The Scramble is the wider event in which the sack of Benin City sits.

reference.author: Hicks, Dan
reference.title: The Brutish Museums: The Benin Bronzes, Colonial Violence and Cultural Restitution
reference.annotation: The work that reframed the Bronzes as evidence of organized colonial violence rather than neutral art with a troubled past. Essential for understanding the contemporary restitution argument and the case that the museum itself is implicated in the original wrong.

reference.author: Plankensteiner, Barbara (ed.)
reference.title: Benin: Kings and Rituals — Court Arts from Nigeria
reference.annotation: The major scholarly catalogue reconstructing the corpus across scattered collections, with essays on court function, casting, and history. The essential reference for the art-historical dimension and for seeing the objects as a coherent body rather than dispersed fragments.

reference.author: Sarr, Felwine; Savoy, Bénédicte
reference.title: The Restitution of African Cultural Heritage: Toward a New Relational Ethics
reference.annotation: The report commissioned by the French president that reset the European restitution debate, arguing that African heritage taken without consent should be returned. Foundational for the policy turn that the Benin case crystallized.

reference.author: Lundén, Staffan
reference.title: Displaying Loot: The Benin Objects and the British Museum
reference.annotation: A detailed study of how the British Museum has presented the Benin objects over time, useful for the specific question of how display conventions foregrounded craft and suppressed the history of the looting.

reference.author: Home, Robert
reference.title: City of Blood Revisited: A New Look at the Benin Expedition of 1897
reference.annotation: An earlier critical account of the punitive expedition that re-examined the British narrative of justified reprisal. Useful for the documented detail of the 1897 events and the contemporary justifications offered for them.

reference.author: Gunsch, Kathryn Wysocki
reference.title: The Benin Plaques: A 16th Century Imperial Monument
reference.annotation: A focused study arguing that the plaques formed a single coordinated program rather than an accumulation over centuries. Useful for the chronology debate and for reconstructing how the plaques may have functioned together as a court record.

commerce.title: The Brutish Museums: The Benin Bronzes, Colonial Violence and Cultural Restitution
commerce.author: Dan Hicks
commerce.note: The book that put the Bronzes at the center of the restitution debate. Hicks tells the story of the 1897 looting and presses the moral case directly, in prose aimed at the general reader who is new to the subject.

commerce.title: Loot: Britain and the Benin Bronzes
commerce.author: Barnaby Phillips
commerce.note: For a reader who wants the full narrative arc, from the sack of Benin City to today's returns. Phillips is a former foreign correspondent and writes a clear, balanced account that gives voice to Nigerian and museum perspectives alike.

commerce.title: Benin: Kings and Rituals — Court Arts from Nigeria
commerce.author: Barbara Plankensteiner (ed.)
commerce.note: Turn here once you want to see the objects as art rather than only as a controversy. This catalogue gathers the scattered corpus with essays on casting, court function, and history, and is the best single volume on the works themselves.

commerce.title: The Restitution of African Cultural Heritage: Toward a New Relational Ethics
commerce.author: Felwine Sarr and Bénédicte Savoy
commerce.note: Read this if you want the argument that shifted European policy. Short and pointed, it lays out the ethical case for return and explains why the Benin material became the test case for the whole debate.

commerce.title: The Benin Plaques: A 16th Century Imperial Monument
commerce.author: Kathryn Wysocki Gunsch
commerce.note: For readers ready for close art-historical analysis. Gunsch argues the plaques formed one coordinated program, and her study is the place to follow the technical debates over chronology and arrangement.

popularCulture.title: The restitution debate
popularCulture.description: The Benin Bronzes are now the most famous example in the global argument about returning looted art. News stories follow each return as a test of whether museums will give things back. The coverage often makes it look like a simple fight between Europe and Nigeria. The harder dispute, over who inside Nigeria should receive the objects, gets far less attention.

popularCulture.title: Television coverage of the returns
popularCulture.description: News programs and documentaries have filmed Bronzes being handed back, often showing crates opened in Nigeria. The images are moving and make the history feel real. They tend to end on the moment of return, as if the story closes there. What follows, the slow questions of custody and care, rarely fits the broadcast.

popularCulture.title: The Edo Museum of West African Art
popularCulture.description: A new museum was announced for Benin City to house returning Bronzes. It became a symbol of homecoming long before it was built. Plans, designs, and fundraising drew wide attention. Construction and the dispute over who controls the objects have proved slower and harder than the hopeful early announcements suggested.

popularCulture.title: Benin Bronzes as a phrase for looted art
popularCulture.description: The name has spread beyond the objects themselves. People now say Benin Bronzes as shorthand for any treasure taken in the colonial era and held abroad. The phrase carries the whole argument in two words. It can also flatten very different cases into one slogan, hiding how much each looted collection differs in its history.

popularCulture.title: Museum labels and how they tell the story
popularCulture.description: For decades museum labels praised the casting and said almost nothing about the burning of Benin City. Visitors admired the skill without learning how the pieces arrived. Some museums have rewritten their labels to name the looting plainly. The change is small on the wall but large in meaning, since it decides what a casual visitor learns.
